content: Regarding former Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli's comments in the media claiming that the police did not fire but that criminal elements fired in a planned manner from the outside, I consider this an entry of baseless and irresponsible commentary from a person of that stature. Such comments from responsible individuals only increase confusion and rumors.

I believe there was infiltration not only by groups dissatisfied with the system and government but also by disgruntled factions within the major parties. No reports were received suggesting that the Armed Police Force personnel deployed on duty in the Valley on September 9 (Bhadra 24) failed to perform their tasks. These accidents occurred because the relevant agencies did not take the information provided by our Department seriously.

In the abnormal circumstances of September 9 (Bhadra 24), the NID has no direct role as it lacks access to the security arrangements of the Valley's prisons. The probability of the incident of prisoners escaping being pre-planned in connection with the protesters' activities seems low. Since there was no pressure from protesters at prisons other than Nakkhu on the 24th, the prison security administration itself appears accountable for the escape of inmates.

It is essential to develop an integrated mechanism for information exchange and analysis among high-level intelligence officers of the NID and the other three security agencies. Furthermore, the system for tracking new emerging security challenges arising via digital platforms must be strengthened. During protests or movements, management can be achieved by establishing an integrated command post consisting of high-level IT officers from all security agencies.

Regarding the events of the 8th (Bhadra 23), it is felt that due to the lack of necessary intelligence equipment and skilled manpower, the NID was unable to provide reporting to the government with the level of precise analysis that was required.

Following the events of the 8th (Bhadra 23), since the security agencies were in a defensive state, the analysis suggested that an attack on the Federal Parliament Building was inevitable. The centers of the Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary were destroyed by arson through plans woven overnight on the foundation of the anger generated by the events of the 8th.

The new generation cannot agree with an entirely traditional mindset; therefore, the state machinery must involve the new generation in every level of policy-making and state-governance opportunities. Additionally, the state must prioritize transparency and good governance. If the middleman system that has taken root in the past can be eradicated and the system itself institutionalized, it appears that relations with the youth generation can be improved alongside institutional reforms in every sector.

If the bill regarding National Intelligence operations is appropriately amended and given legal form to strengthen and professionalize the NID, the organization can become strong, professional, and competitive with organizations from other countries. For the immediate term, if legal support for the formation and operation of the intelligence mechanism, along with skilled manpower development, logistical support, and necessary budget arrangements are made, the organization would have the opportunity to prove its effectiveness. Furthermore, modern telephone tapping equipment brought at great expense remains idle due to a lack of legal framework; its operation alone would have enabled the acquisition of necessary information in the field of national security.
5.2. Statements of the Organizers and Incident Report Details:
126. Statement of Sudan Gurung:

I operate an apartment business, an IT company, and work as an Adventure Coach; I am also affiliated with the organization 'Hami Nepal.' Hami Nepal is a non-profit social organization registered at the Office of the Company Registrar, Tripureshwor (Reg No. 241179/077/078). Since its establishment, it has been involved in relief and rescue during the 2015 earthquake and subsequent landslides. We managed the entire operation of the Trauma Center hospital and coordinated with management at TU Teaching Hospital during the COVID-19 pandemic. During the second wave, we established and operated 270 beds at the COVID Unified Hospital (Bir Hospital) within three days. We distributed portable X-ray machines to the Trauma Center, saved 57 patients on high-flow oxygen in various hospitals, and sent 14 tons of relief materials to Turkey in coordination with the Nepal government during their earthquake. I have served as the Chairman of 'Hami Nepal' since its registration.

Under Section 4 of our charter, motivated by transparency and accountability in a country facing extreme corruption, we applied to the District Administration Office, Kathmandu on September 7, 2025 (Bhadra 22), for legal permission to hold a peaceful protest at Maitighar Mandala on September 8 (Bhadra 23). Our volunteers gathered at 7:00 AM with first-aid kits and banners reading 'YOUTH AGAINST CORRUPTION.' We organized protesters into groups of 20 with designated leaders to maintain discipline and prevent anarchist elements from infiltrating. On the day of the Gen-Z protest, a business named 'Chulho Daura' provided 80 cases of mineral water, which we distributed. A day prior, we received information through a separate Discord group about potential anarchist activities, including the construction of Molotov cocktails and plans for arson and looting. Anil Baniya and I personally informed the then-Police Chief of Kathmandu and the Cyber Bureau at Bhotahity about these threats. While we were at Maitighar around 9:00 AM, a group of 55 people led by Raksha Bam arrived and told us, 'This is not your protest, stay silent.' We handed over our microphone and speakers to them.

As the crowd grew, our volunteers repeatedly requested them to stay calm. To prevent escalation, we stood at the front with banners. We climbed onto a vehicle and urged protesters to remain disciplined. Raksha Bam also gave a speech from the vehicle, saying 'We won't throw stones today, we won't hit anyone.' However, the crowd soon began shouting 'We are going towards Everest Hotel' and started moving. We tried to control them using the vehicle, but the mass surged toward Baneshwor. I was in the vehicle with PK Khanal (current Minister of Youth and Sports), Raksha Bam, Purushottam Yadav, and Bablu Thapa. When protesters began tearing down photos of the then-Prime Minister from utility poles, we tried to stop them, but they continued. The crowd briefly stopped for slogans but was pushed forward by the mass behind them. Despite our volunteers' efforts to hold the line at the Everest Hotel barricade, the crowd breached it. I personally warned the youth repeatedly not to move toward the Parliament Building, which was a restricted zone. I even submitted a pen drive containing videos I made for my social media platforms advising students and youth on safety measures. Most of us were unaware of the curfew declaration because security forces had already started using tear gas, water cannons, and batons, and injuries had begun. Our team focused entirely on rescue operations, taking the injured to Civil Hospital and our medical camp at Maitighar. The noise was so great that police announcements via megaphone were inaudible to the protesters. I only learned about the deaths from gunfire once my team informed me.

While visiting the injured at Civil Hospital, I saw a police team aiming long guns at protesters inside the hospital. Dr. Toshima Karki (then MP) was also present. Together with hospital doctors and Hami Nepal volunteers, we requested the security forces to stay outside to prevent further conflict. About 15-20 of our volunteers guarded the main gate. Around 3:00 PM, I saw a 19-year-old girl in college dress who had been hit by tear gas smoke in the eyes, and another protester covered in blood from a head injury being carried by five people to Civil Hospital. Regarding the Discord platform, we had clearly announced on our official Instagram that any talk of violence or arson would result in a permanent ban and reporting to the police. Hami Nepal received no outside funding for the September 8-9 protests; I personally spent 50,000 rupees from my own funds, for which I have submitted screenshots.

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I went to Civil Hospital at 7:00 AM to check on the injured. Later, from our office in Ranibari, I coordinated blood donations and mobilized volunteers. After the then-Prime Minister resigned, I went to the Parliament Building, but arson had already occurred. We tried to prevent further damage and then moved to Maitighar. Hearing reports of fire at Singhadurbar, we rushed there but found protesters had already entered. We then moved to a hotel near Baluwatar where two people were reportedly trapped; we helped clear furniture to create a path and urged protesters not to burn it. When I reached the Prime Minister's residence, the main gate was on fire. I then received a call stating that the Chief of Army Staff (COAS) wanted to meet at the Army HQ in Bhadrakali. I believe the nationwide chaos was a result of spontaneous citizen anger and retaliation for the killing of unarmed students and youth at the Parliament Building on the 23rd. On the evening of the 24th, while celebrating the PM's resignation near the Traffic Police Office, we urged protesters not to engage in arson. My team and I played no part in the violence; in fact, the IGP and SSP Narayan Datta Paudel called me asking to help protect the Police HQ. We mobilized volunteers via Discord to form human chains and protect both the Police and Army HQs. We collaborated with Army Officer Bhola Thapa to run a helpline and provide information to the Army throughout the night when police presence was zero.

During the second wave of COVID, Dr. Sanduk Ruit contacted me to offer support. I requested office space, and he provided rooms at the Barbara Foundation office in Baluwatar. We have no other affiliation or financial ties with the Barbara Foundation. I have submitted our audit reports and bank statements for the last three months as proof. For the rule of law to prevail, the government must ensure stability and accountability to prevent such tragedies from recurring.
127. Statement of Raksha Bam:

I am a Master's student at K&K College, New Baneshwor, and I previously worked at the Ujwal Thapa Foundation. Information about a protest on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), demanding good governance and anti-corruption measures, was circulating widely on social media. Many groups supported the call. As a Gen-Z individual, I decided to participate. I discussed this with various Gen-Z groups, eventually meeting at Maitighar. While Miraj Dhungana's group had differing views and wanted to protest only after 6:00 PM, the rest of us agreed to gather at 9:00 AM, march to the Everest Hotel in Baneshwor as permitted by the DAO, and return to Maitighar for conclusion. On the morning of the 23rd, Sudan Gurung's group was already managing the site when I arrived. The crowd was massive. Around 10:30 AM, we began the march. We reached Baneshwor around 11:30 AM. Protesters at the front reached the barricades. I heard that scuffles broke out between protesters and security forces. Later, I heard the barricades were breached. Protesters expressed anger when security forces pointed weapons at them. While some urged restraint, the sheer volume of people broke the line. Reports came that people were trying to enter the Parliament Building. I heard gunfire. Realizing the situation was worsening, I urged students in uniform to take alternative routes home. By 1:00 or 2:00 PM, my friends and I moved to Anamnagar for safety. Baneshwor was like a battlefield. In the evening, I learned of the high number of casualties. The public was furious at the state's use of lethal force.

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), protests broke out nationwide. I was near the Parliament Building in Baneshwor. The crowd was trying to enter, and security forces fired from inside; I saw an unarmed protester killed right in front of me. This further enraged the crowd. They began vandalizing the building. While some of us tried to stop the arson, the crowd was beyond listening. Eventually, the Parliament Building was set on fire. The mob then targeted Singhadurbar, courts, and commercial establishments. This violence was a direct result of the state's failure to manage the peaceful protest on the 23rd and the subsequent killing of youth. There were also intoxicated individuals in the crowd who were aggressive. I also saw people identifying themselves as pro-monarchists. I believe all political parties had representation there. On the 24th, I even saw an intoxicated man with a gun acting recklessly. I hope this commission investigates potential infiltration. The state must provide compensation and respect to the families of the martyrs and the injured. Those responsible for the killings must be punished, as should those involved in destructive arson.
128. Statement of Khemraj Saud:

I am a student at Pashupati Multiple Campus and run a business called Badimalika Agriculture Company Pvt. Ltd. I am the founding chairman of 'Deshbhakta Yuva Nepal,' a non-profit that has campaigned for justice for Nirmala Panta, cooperative victims, and against the MCC, SPP, and the Citizenship Bill. We operate on personal contributions. I joined a Discord group on September 5, 2025 (Bhadra 20), following the corruption and 'Nepo Baby' trends. Ankit Malla and I sought written permission from the DAO to protest at Maitighar. I did not know Sudan Gurung or Purushottam Yadav before this. On the 23rd, I arrived at Maitighar at 9:00 AM. Our group of about 400-500 people wore black masks as planned. We carried banners against corruption and the social media ban. As the crowd grew, I spoke from a vehicle, emphasizing that Gen-Z is an intellectual generation and we should not destroy property or target individuals. We planned to march to the restricted zone, hold a corner meeting, and return. The road from Maitighar to Bijuli Bazar was packed. At Everest Hotel, I noticed the security presence was weakfew personnel and only a single line of barricades. The commander's address to the crowd was harsh. After some scuffling, the police retreated. We tried to hold the crowd, but police began using water cannons and tear gas, causing the crowd to surge forward. The road to the Parliament Building was suddenly empty as police retreated inside. Protesters surrounded the building. When gas was fired from inside, the crowd became enraged and started climbing the walls. I didn't notice exactly when the curfew began. I tried to call the CDO, but couldn't connect. Suddenly, bullets were fired. I was near the south gate; a protester next to me was shot. He begged me to save him. Three of us carried him to Civil Hospital, where he was declared dead. I rescued three people in total that day. At the south gate, I saw someone with hands raised being shot. Police were targeting protesters. At the hospital, police blocked relatives from entering and treated me rudely. I left for a friend's place in Bhaisepati that night.

On the 24th, I stayed at the room until 4:00 PM, watching the arson on social media. I posted appeals to not burn national heritage. Later, I met friends at Sundhara; we discussed the destruction of the judiciary, executive, and parliament. When we heard rumors of a plan to burn Pashupatinath temple, we rushed there to protect it, but found it safe and informed the Army. Later, we heard the Army wanted to talk. We met at the 'Hami Nepal' office and some went to Bhadrakali. That night, we received calls from girls' hostels about strangers at their doors; we alerted the Army. We discussed who should be the next PM. While Balen Shah was the first choice, he wasn't interested. We then approached Sushila Karki due to her legal expertise; she agreed, and the Gen-Z group recommended her name to the Army Chief. I believe there was no infiltration on the 23rdthe violence on the 24th was a reaction to the killing of 19 people. However, the specific acts of looting weapons and killing police officers likely involved elements with malicious intent.
129. Statement of Ronesh Pradhan:

I run Radhika Suppliers in Thulo Bharyang. I have long volunteered for 'Hami Nepal.' Following the social media promotion of the Gen-Z protest on September 8 (Bhadra 23), our team went to Maitighar with doctors, medicine, and an ambulance around 9:00 AM. The crowd was immense. We moved with the protesters toward Baneshwor. At the Everest Hotel barricades, the crowd tried to breach the line despite police warnings. We also urged them not to break the barricades, but they wouldn't listen and even turned on us. Intoxicated individuals were inciting the crowd to push. When the crowd pelted water bottles, police used water cannons and tear gas. The mass eventually broke through and became aggressive, throwing stones at the Parliament Building. Then, gunfire started. I saw a student in a Global College uniform get shot in the head; his brains were visible. I took him and two other injured people to the Trauma Center. The student died on the way. On the 24th, I assisted the injured at the Trauma Center. I heard about the fire at Singhadurbar but it was too late to help. At the Hotel Museum in Baluwatar, we heard people were trapped, but they had already been evacuated. Some were looting items from the hotel. I spent the rest of the day helping with evacuations at the Hilton Hotel.

On the 23rd, I saw intoxicated individuals inciting others. I believe the massive destruction of government and private property on the 24th occurred because anarchist elements took advantage of the Gen-Z protest to infiltrate and cause damage.
5.2. Statements of the Organizers and Incident Report Details:
126. Statement of Sudan Gurung:

I operate an apartment business, an IT company, and work as an Adventure Coach; I am also affiliated with the organization 'Hami Nepal.' Hami Nepal is a non-profit social organization registered at the Office of the Company Registrar, Tripureshwor (Reg No. 241179/077/078). Since its establishment, it has been involved in relief and rescue during the 2015 earthquake and subsequent landslides. We managed the entire operation of the Trauma Center hospital and coordinated with management at TU Teaching Hospital during the COVID-19 pandemic. While visiting the injured at Civil Hospital, I saw a police team aiming long guns at protesters inside the hospital. Dr. Toshima Karki (then MP) was also present. Together with hospital doctors and Hami Nepal volunteers, we requested the security forces to stay outside to prevent further conflict. About 15-20 of our volunteers guarded the main gate. Around 3:00 PM, I saw a 19-year-old girl in college dress who had been hit by tear gas smoke in the eyes, and another protester covered in blood from a head injury being carried by five people to Civil Hospital. Regarding the Discord platform, we had clearly announced on our official Instagram that any talk of violence or arson would result in a permanent ban and reporting to the police. Hami Nepal received no outside funding for the September 8-9 protests; I personally spent 50,000 rupees from my own funds, for which I have submitted screenshots.

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I went to Civil Hospital at 7:00 AM to check on the injured. Later, from our office in Ranibari, I coordinated blood donations and mobilized volunteers. After the then-Prime Minister resigned, I went to the Parliament Building, but arson had already occurred. We tried to prevent further damage and then moved to Maitighar. Hearing reports of fire at Singhadurbar, we rushed there but found protesters had already entered. We then moved to a hotel near Baluwatar where two people were reportedly trapped; we helped clear furniture to create a path and urged protesters not to burn it. When I reached the Prime Minister's residence, the main gate was on fire. I then received a call stating that the Chief of Army Staff (COAS) wanted to meet at the Army HQ in Bhadrakali. I believe the nationwide chaos was a result of spontaneous citizen anger and retaliation for the killing of unarmed students and youth at the Parliament Building on the 23rd. My team and I played no part in the violence; in fact, the IGP and SSP Narayan Datta Paudel called me asking to help protect the Police HQ. We mobilized volunteers via Discord to form human chains and protect both the Police and Army HQs. We collaborated with Army Officer Bhola Thapa to run a helpline and provide information to the Army throughout the night when police presence was zero.

During the second wave of COVID, Dr. Sanduk Ruit contacted me to offer support. I requested office space, and he provided rooms at the Barbara Foundation office in Baluwatar. We have no other affiliation or financial ties with the Barbara Foundation. I have submitted our audit reports and bank statements for the last three months as proof. For the rule of law to prevail, the government must ensure stability and accountability to prevent such tragedies from recurring.
127. Statement of Raksha Bam:

I am a Master's student at K&K College, New Baneshwor, and I previously worked at the Ujwal Thapa Foundation in Panipokhari. Information about a protest on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), demanding good governance, national interest, and anti-corruption measures, was circulating widely on social media. Various social media groups were supporting the movement. As a Gen-Z individual, I decided to participate and contribute. Subsequently, I and other acquaintances discussed this with various Gen-Z groups. Our final meeting was held in the open space of Maitighar, Kathmandu. Among them, Miraj Dhungana's group had slightly different views and stated they would only protest at 6:00 PM (18:00) after the other Gen-Z groups had finished. During the discussions, it was decided to gather at Maitighar at 9:00 AM, march to the Everest Hotel in Baneshwor as permitted by the District Administration Office, and then return to Maitighar to conclude the program peacefully. Accordingly, I arrived at Maitighar around 5:00 AM on September 8 (Bhadra 23). Sudan Gurung's group was already there managing the site. Protesters continued to gather, and the crowd grew so large that Maitighar Mandala could no longer contain it. Around 10:30 AM, the march began from Maitighar. We reached Baneshwor around 11:30 AM. I was slightly behind, while the protesters at the front had reached the barricades placed by security forces near the Federal Parliament Building to mark the restricted zone. While the plan was to return to Maitighar from there, I heard that scuffles broke out between the front-line protesters and security forces; shortly after, I heard the barricades were breached. I later understood that when protesters reached the barricades and expressed their anger, security forces began pointing weapons, leading to a push-and-pull. In the process, the massive crowd broke the barricades, though some protesters had urged others not to push and even asked security forces to reset the barriers. Soon, news arrived that protesters had moved past the restricted zone to Baneshwor Chowk and were attempting to enter the Parliament Building. The sound of gunfire began to erupt. The situation became increasingly complex. At that time, a wounded protester came running by. Realizing the situation was deteriorating, I urged students in school uniforms to use alternative routes to go home. Some returned home, while others remained in the movement. Protesters were throwing stones, and security forces were firing indiscriminately. By around 1:00–2:00 PM, some friends and I moved to Anamnagar for safety. By evening, the Baneshwor area resembled a battlefield. Once things calmed down, I learned that a large number of protesters had been killed or injured by security fire. The public was extremely enraged by the state's lethal suppression on September 8 (Bhadra 23). I felt the people were restless, waiting for dawn. Due to the events of the 23rd, nationwide protests began on the morning of September 9 (Bhadra 24). That day, I was near the Parliament Building in Baneshwor. The crowd was massive and attempting to enter; again, security forces fired from inside, and an unarmed protester was killed right in front of me. After the killing, the protesters became beyond furious. They attempted to enter the Parliament Building by vandalizing it, and security forces used equal force. Some of us tried to advise against vandalism and arson, but the protesters were in a state where they wouldn't listen to anyone. Eventually, they vandalized and set fire to the Parliament Building. On that day, mobs also caused damage by vandalizing and setting fire to main administrative bodies at Singhadurbar, judicial bodies including courts, government offices, and residences of high-ranking political figures and commercial establishments. The arson and vandalism on the 24th were the result of the public anger caused by the state's failure to manage a peaceful protest and the killing of many youths on the 23rd. There were many intoxicated individuals in the protest who would turn on us when we tried to advise them. I also saw people identifying as pro-monarchists. I believe there was participation from all political parties. On the 24th, I even saw an intoxicated man in Baneshwor with a gun acting recklessly. I am hopeful that this commission will investigate whether any groups or individuals infiltrated to incite the protest and take legal action. The state must provide high honors and proper livelihood management for the families of the martyrs and the injured. I demand legal punishment for those responsible for the killings and for those involved in destructive acts like arson and vandalism.
128. Statement of Khemraj Saud:

I am a Bachelor's student at Pashupati Multiple Campus, Chabahil, and I also run a business called Badimalika Agriculture Company Pvt. Ltd. I have served as the founding chairman of a non-profit organization called 'Deshbhakta Yuva Nepal.' This organization has worked against social evils and served individuals and communities in need. Through this organization, we campaigned for justice for Nirmala Panta, for victims of cooperatives, and against the MCC, SPP, and the Citizenship Bill, as well as for the release of Bipin Joshi. The organization is run through the personal contributions of its members. During this time, trends regarding widespread corruption and 'Nepo Babies' were circulating on Facebook and TikTok. Expressing solidarity with these voices, I joined a Discord group on the night of September 5, 2025 (Bhadra 20). Information was circulating that a protest would be held in front of the Parliament Building at 12:00 PM on September 8 (Bhadra 23); however, as the Parliament is a restricted zone, we agreed to protest at the state-designated site, Maitighar Mandala. Ankit Malla and I sought written permission from the District Administration Office, Kathmandu. I had no prior acquaintance or consultation with Sudan Gurung or Purushottam Yadav regarding this. I reached Maitighar around 9:00 AM on September 8 (Bhadra 23). There were about 400–500 members from our 'Discord 03' group, though members do not personally know each other. We had agreed to wear black masks. We participated with banners saying 'End Corruption,' 'Social Media Cannot Be Banned,' and 'End Nepotism and Favoritism.' Other groups were also participating. As the crowd grew, I climbed onto a vehicle equipped with a megaphone and announced that 'Gen-Z is an intellectual and capable generation; as conscious citizens, we do not break trees or litter, we should not target individuals with slander, and we will protest against irregularities and corruption with restraint.' We had a general plan to march to the restricted zone, hold a corner meeting, and return to Maitighar. I was on the vehicle leading slogans. The rally filled the entire road from Maitighar to the Bijuli Bazar bridge. At Everest Hotel, police had placed barricades, but from what I saw, the security cordon was not strong. The number of personnel was low, and the barricade was only a single line. Furthermore, the police commander failed to address the crowd effectively and spoke quite harshly. Because a crowd contains people with various motives, a minor scuffle occurred, and the police retreated. We repeatedly urged the crowd not to move forward and held the line for a while. However, after the police continuously used water cannons, batons, and tear gas, the protesters ignored our pleas and moved forward. The police simultaneously retreated inside the Parliament Building. The road was completely empty; there was no obstruction from security forces up to the Parliament. Protesters surrounded the building. After tear gas was fired continuously from inside, the crowd became further enraged, and some began climbing the main gates and walls. I didn't realize when the curfew was imposed. We continued to ask the crowd not to engage in reckless activity. I tried to call the CDO to report the situation, but the call wouldn't connect. Suddenly, gunfire started. I was near the south gate of the Parliament; a protester next to me was shot and collapsed. Hearing him say 'Brother, save me' left me deeply distraught. Three of us carried him to the nearby Civil Hospital, where doctors informed us he had already passed away. While at the hospital, the influx of injured people increased. I returned to the Parliament area. I saw another protester fall from a gunshot and helped rescue him to the hospital. In total, I rescued three people. The last time I reached the south gate, someone was lying shot, and while another person was pleading with hands joined not to fire, that person was also shot. That day, the police were taking aim and shooting at protesters. After taking that person to the hospital, the police didn't even let us out. Police were deployed at the hospital gates, preventing relatives from entering. The police inside the hospital even behaved rudely toward me. Around 9:00 PM (21:00), I left the hospital and went to the Trauma Center after hearing of more casualties, but the police blocked our entry. I then went to a friend's room in Bhaisepati, Lalitpur, to sleep. There was no conversation on Discord that night. I stayed there until 4:00 PM (16:00) the next day. I learned of the arson and vandalism on the 24th through social media. I appealed via social media that national heritage like the Parliament and Singhadurbar should not be burned. After 4:00 PM, I and friends like Junal Gadal and Pawan Gautam entered the city to see the situation. Seeing Singhadurbar and the Supreme Court on fire, we gathered at Sundhara and discussed the destruction of the Judiciary, Executive, and Parliament, the PM's resignation, and how to move forward. In the evening, hearing rumors that the Pashupatinath temple was being targeted, we rushed there to protect it but found the situation normal and informed the Army. After hearing that the Army had called the protesters for talks, we gathered at the 'Hami Nepal' office in Ranibari, and some went to Bhadrakali. That night, we received calls from various girls' hostels saying unknown individuals were at their doors; we informed the Army. Discussions continued regarding the next Prime Minister. While Balen Shah was the first choice, he showed no interest. Given the critical situation, we approached Sushila Karki due to her legal expertise, and after she agreed, the Gen-Z group recommended her name to the Chief of Army Staff.

There was no infiltration in the protest on September 8 (Bhadra 23). However, the brutal suppression by the government, where 19 people were killed by gunfire within three hours during a peaceful protest, caused the public anger that brought people to the streets on the 24th. I believe that while the initial anger was spontaneous, the massive destruction of administrative bodies, the Supreme Court, private property, and police stations, as well as the killing of police and looting of weapons, involved infiltration by individuals with vested interests.
129. Statement of Ronesh Pradhan:

I run Radhika Suppliers (chocolate business) in Thulo Bharyang, Kathmandu. I have been volunteering for the organization 'Hami Nepal' during disasters for a long time. There was widespread social media promotion regarding a protest by the Gen-Z generation on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), for national interest, good governance, and anti-corruption; however, the leadership was unknown. On that morning, Gen-Z youths gathered at Maitighar. Accordingly, a team from 'Hami Nepal' with doctors, medicine, and an ambulance reached Maitighar around 9:00–10:00 AM. We were there as volunteers. By 10:00–11:00 AM, the crowd was so massive that Maitighar could not contain it. The protesters began marching toward Baneshwor. We moved with them. At Everest Hotel, barricades marked the restricted zone around the Parliament. Security personnel were deployed there. The mob tried to break the barricades while security forces tried to explain the restriction. Protesters ignored them and began pushing. A long scuffle ensued. We also requested them not to break the barriers, but they wouldn't listen and even tried to attack us. Intoxicated protesters were inciting the crowd to push. The mob began throwing water bottles at the police. Security forces tried to control the crowd with water cannons, tear gas, and batons. The massive crowd could not be controlled and breached the barricades, moving toward the Parliament. Emboldened, the protesters began pelting stones at security forces in an aggressive attempt to enter the Parliament. When water cannons and tear gas failed to stop them, gunfire began. Protesters began falling with gunshot wounds. I saw a student in a Global College uniform get shot in the head, with brain matter exposed. I took him and two others to the National Trauma Center in an ambulance. The Global College student died on the way. The next morning, September 9 (Bhadra 24), I went to the Trauma Center to assist the injured. I heard Singhadurbar was on fire and went there, but it was beyond control. At the Hotel Museum in Baluwatar, we heard two people were trapped; we rushed there but learned they had already been rescued. People were seen looting items from the hotel. In the evening, we heard people were trapped at the Hilton Hotel, but they had been rescued by the time we arrived. The day ended while we were involved in rescue efforts.

Regarding the protests on the 23rd and 24th: On the 23rd, we saw many intoxicated individuals who were highly aggressive and inciting others. Regarding the vandalism and arson at the Parliament, Singhadurbar, government offices, and private residences on the 24th, I believe anarchist groups took advantage of the Gen-Z protest to infiltrate and cause massive loss of life and property.
130. Statement of Associate Vishal Shrestha:

I run Daisy Travels & Adventure in Kupondole, Lalitpur. I have been a long-time volunteer for 'Hami Nepal.' Following the social media promotion of the Gen-Z protest on September 8 (Bhadra 23), our team went to Maitighar with medical supplies and an ambulance around 9:00–10:00 AM. By 11:00 AM, the crowd was immense. We marched with them to Baneshwor. At the Everest Hotel barricades, we witnessed the scuffle between protesters and security forces. We urged the protesters not to break the barriers, but they were aggressive and turned on us. Intoxicated individuals were inciting the mob. After the crowd breached the barricades and targeted the Parliament Building with stones, gunfire broke out. I assisted in taking the injured to Civil Hospital and TU Teaching Hospital. The next morning, I assisted at the Trauma Center. I witnessed the uncontrollable fire at Singhadurbar and later helped at the Hotel Museum in Baluwatar. In the evening, while attempting rescue work at Bhatbhateni in Bouddha, I was injured by intoxicated individuals who hit me on the head. We rescued people trapped in that Bhatbhateni. I believe the widespread destruction on the 24th was caused by anarchist elements who infiltrated the Gen-Z protest.

Regarding the movement on September 8 and 9 (Bhadra 23 and 24): On the 23rd, we saw the participation of individuals who had consumed alcohol or drugs, acting very aggressively and inciting other protesters. Likewise, the vandalism and arson at the Federal Parliament, Singhadurbar, major administrative bodies, government offices, commercial establishments, and private residences on the 24th seem to be the result of anarchist groups taking advantage of the Gen-Z protest to infiltrate and cause massive loss of life and property.
131. Statement of Rishi Thapa, Protest Participant and Helper:

Following the government's ban on social media, the Gen-Z (2020-2) group extensively promoted a protest against state corruption for Bhadra 23, 2082. As a member of that generation, I supported the movement through Rakshya Bam's group. I prepared slogans criticizing leaders like Prithvi Subba Gurung, Ramesh Lekhak, and Sher Bahadur Deuba, which I shared with a friend, Aditya Karna, via Viber. On the day of the protest, I arrived at Baneshwar around 11:30 AM with Tshering Tamang. By the time we reached Everest Hospital, protesters had already breached the barricades. At the South Gate of the Parliament building, I personally tried to maintain order, telling people, 'This is not our motive, please sit down,' and we began singing national songs like 'Rato Ra Chandra Surya.'

The situation escalated when a protester named Yadav and others climbed the gate. Police responded with tear gas and batons. As firing began, I saw protesters hit in the head and torso; the bloodshed was immense. We used national flags as bandages to stop the bleeding and crammed 5–10 injured people into each ambulance. My friend Sulav Raj Shrestha was shot in the head and died instantly beside me. We were chased into alleys where police continued firing. Later, I saw a person shot in the chest who died at Civil Hospital. I rescued approximately 20–22 people with horrific injuries, including missing limbs, exposed ribs, and a shot to the eye. Despite my friends Suresh Magar and Amrit Thapa trying to take me home, I stayed, inspired by our ancestors like Bhimsen Thapa. By 5:45 PM, I took a neck-wound victim to Civil Hospital on a motorcycle. My clothes were soaked in blood. The next day, I joined protests in Panauti. I believe the movement was a spontaneous outburst against corruption and 'Nepo Babies,' though I suspect the 'TOB' group and others on the 24th may have been infiltrators or anti-democratic elements.
132. Statement of Purushottam Subhaprabhat Yadav:

I am an unemployed journalism graduate. I participated in the anti-corruption movement as a concerned citizen, not as part of an organized group. Sabal Gautam and I had even requested written permission from the District Administration Office for a peaceful protest. On Bhadra 23, I reached Maitighar around 8:00 AM. The protest was peaceful, featuring slogans like 'Wake Up Nepal' and 'Say No to Corruption.' As the crowd grew, we decided to march to the Baneshwar restricted zone and return to Maitighar for a hunger strike. Security was initially thin, but as the crowd became aggressive and breached the single barricade, I realized things were getting out of control. My colleague K.P. Khanal and I sought safety inside a nearby college building. When I heard gunfire, I issued a press release via social media and newspapers, stating that the protest had been infiltrated and urging everyone to go home. We stayed hidden in the college all night for safety. I maintain that our intent was peaceful, but the event was hijacked by interest groups.
